change_summary: EXP-228 integrated three targeted edits: (1) finding field replaced to concede dome's scaling equation + FSF derivation while keeping critique on absolute-magnitude observation-anchoring; (2) P1 sentence 4 replaced to fix internal contradiction with P2 (no first-principles equation that derives magnitude, not no equation at all); (3) P7 block replaced to reframe 2026-08-12 discriminator from coverage (which both models share) to geographic-latitude FSF vs magnetic-latitude scaling.
our_argument_summary: WIN-010 argues the BOU 2017 -10.9 nT eclipse signal is fully explained by Chapman (1933) Sq-current physics as quantitatively extended by Kim & Chang (2018), that the dome's geometric FSF scaling between stations is genuinely derived (conceded) but anchors absolute magnitude to the very observation it claims to predict (observation-anchored, not dome-derived), that the dome's monitor.py imposes an asymmetric Kp<2 test gate relative to the Kp~=3 baseline it accepts, and that the 2026-08-12 European eclipse provides a clean external test of geographic-latitude FSF versus magnetic-latitude scaling.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: Dome formula delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF with FSF = H(r)/r normalized to BOU. H(r) = 8537 × exp(-r/8619) km. r is solved from r × tan(lat) = H(r). Baseline = -10.9 nT from 2017 BOU observation. Coverage from Besselian elements. monitor.py (per prior cycles) hardcodes the -10.9 nT target on the same commit as the INTERMAGNET fetch line, with Kp<2 gate for 2026 validation.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Hardcoded target value (-10.9 nT) on same commit as the fetch — per c5/c6 validation. FSF values in raw-text/03-predictions.txt are computed (I reproduced them from V12 geometry with Pearson r = 0.9999).
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: Kp < 2 precondition on 2026 validation; the 2017 baseline itself was recorded at Kp ~= 3, creating an asymmetric evidentiary standard still correctly flagged in P4.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None newly introduced by EXP-228. Still-outstanding c6 Hole 4 (0.1%/1.8% quantitative precision) was deliberately deferred by EXP-228 — see holes_found below.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Per c5/c6 validation: monitor.py compares a hardcoded -10.9 nT target against the fetched INTERMAGNET BOU 2017 minute-averaged Z-component and applies a Kp<2 gate for 2026. Target baked on the same commit as the fetch line. EXP-228 did not change any monitoring/code behavior — only detail_verdict_text prose and the finding field.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Chapman (1933) + Kim & Chang (2018) predict the BOU 2017 signal magnitude and geometry without dome parameters. BOU sits at magnetic latitude ~48° N, inside the Kim & Chang 30°-50° response zone. EXP-228 did not alter this tag or its evidence.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: INTERMAGNET BOU 2017 archive was publicly available before the dome monitor commit. No pre-registration in a timestamping venue independent of the author. Under the dome's own definition (prediction timestamped before confirming data fetched), the monitor hardcodes the target on the same commit as the fetch. EXP-228 did not touch P5 (post-hoc paragraph).
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: FSF station-to-station ratio IS derived (Pearson r = 0.999912 between claimed FSF and independently-computed H(r)/r from V12 — see quantitative_verification below). But FSF is a scaling ratio between stations; the absolute magnitude at Boulder (-10.9 nT) is imported from the 2017 observation, not derived. Tag remains false. EXP-228 correctly tightened the finding to match this nuance.

holes_found.description: P7 reframing introduces an asymmetric test setup: the dome side has nine specific published FSF numbers (EBR 1.029, SPT 1.001, ESK 1.722, LER 2.075, HAD 1.471, NGK 1.529, SNK 0.642, CLF 1.325, COI 1.008) but the magnetic-latitude comparator is only described as 'consistent with Kim & Chang's 30°-50° response zone' with no specific per-station predictions. A dome defender can point out that after 2026 data arrives, any observed residual pattern can be pronounced 'consistent with Kim & Chang' because we never pinned the competing prediction down. This weakens the 'clean external test' framing the paragraph builds. New hole introduced by the EXP-228 rewrite — the old P7 was wrong (coverage is not the discriminator) but it at least had both sides equally specified.
holes_found.recommendation: Add one clarifying sentence to P7 along the lines of: 'We will construct the reference magnetic-latitude prediction by fitting Kim & Chang (2018)'s 39-eclipse Y-component response as a smooth function of magnetic latitude and evaluating at each station's magnetic coordinate — or, barring access to the raw ensemble, using the published 30°-50° response envelope with the station's magnetic latitude inside or outside the zone as the predicted signal-present/suppressed flag.' This closes the 'any outcome is consistent with Kim & Chang' loophole by committing to a specific evaluation method before 2026 data arrives.

holes_found.description: P7 residual formulation '(observed delta_Z) / (baseline × coverage)' assumes BOTH models apply coverage as a simple multiplicative factor. The dome does (explicitly: delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF). But standard Sq physics does not treat coverage as a bare linear multiplier — it enters the ionospheric-conductivity integral in a more complex way (partial obscuration at varying altitudes, local-time dependence, E-region geometry). By residualizing out coverage with a linear division, the review is implicitly adopting the dome's formulation of what standard physics predicts. A rigorous defender could argue we are biasing the residual in the dome's favor by assuming a linear-coverage decomposition that the standard model does not actually endorse.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) add a hedge: 'under the simplest linear-coverage approximation for both models' — this at least signals the assumption, or (b) verify whether Kim & Chang (2018) or a follow-on paper actually supports linear-coverage scaling at mid-latitudes, and cite it if so. Option (b) is stronger but requires a literature check.

holes_found.description: P2 quantitative claim 'reproduces the dome's nine published FSF values (0.642-2.075) to better than 0.1% at seven of nine stations and within 1.8% at the worst' was NOT changed by EXP-228 (it is not in the patch anchors) but remains inaccurate per independent recomputation. Using the dome's equation r × tan(lat) = 8537 × exp(-r/8619) with IAGA station latitudes, I reproduce: 1/9 at <0.1%, 7/9 at <0.5%, 8/9 at <1.0%, max error ~2.16% at SPT (San Pablo, claimed FSF 1.001 vs computed 0.979). Pearson r = 0.999912, which rounds to the claimed 0.9999 and is the strongest correctly-cited quantitative statement in the paragraph. The '0.1%/1.8%' precision claim is overstated by a factor of ~5 on the 'seven of nine' figure and by 0.36 percentage points on the worst case. This is the same issue c6 Hole 4 flagged and that EXP-228 explicitly deferred to a follow-up patch.
holes_found.recommendation: Follow-up micro-patch (separate EXP) to replace 'better than 0.1% at seven of nine stations and within 1.8% at the worst' with 'within 0.5% at eight of nine stations and within 2.2% at the worst' — or soften to 'within approximately 1% at most stations'. Decider should queue this follow-up; it's a two-word swap, no conflict risk.

holes_found.description: c6 Hole 3 (moderate in c6, but with the rewrite shifting the rhetorical weight, I'm downgrading to minor here) remains: P3 asserts 'any smooth monotonic H(r) combined with the dome's r·tan(θ) = H(r) convention produces a latitude-dependent ratio of the same form' without in-text demonstration. EXP-228 deliberately left this for a follow-up EXP. Still a genuine weakness that a capable defender will notice (see advocate_mode below).
holes_found.recommendation: Follow-up EXP adding either a one-sentence empirical footnote (refit a linear or power-law H(r) to the same sun/moon altitude calibration targets and show a similar FSF profile) or softening the 'any H(r)' to 'a broad family of H(r) functions fitted to sun/moon altitude targets produces qualitatively similar FSF patterns'. Decider should queue.

stronger_arguments.description: The review could strengthen P7's test setup by computing the specific magnetic latitudes of the nine stations and the expected Kim & Chang ensemble amplitudes at each mag-lat. The IAGA station registry gives geodetic and CGM magnetic coordinates, and Kim & Chang publish median Y/X/Z amplitudes binned by mag-lat. Precomputing a reference table of expected standard-physics delta_Z per station would make the pre-registered 2026 test fully symmetric (dome's nine FSF numbers vs. standard's nine mag-lat numbers).
sources: Kim & Chang 2018 Adv. Space Res. 61:2040-2054 (39-eclipse ensemble tables)

stronger_arguments.description: The Kp asymmetry argument in P4 could be tightened by citing the specific Kp timeline for 2017-Aug-21 (3-hour Kp values for the eclipse window) from the NOAA/GFZ Potsdam Kp index archive. The current text says 'Kp ~= 3' without a reference; a precise citation would harden the claim that the 2017 baseline itself would have voided the dome's own 2026 precondition.

kernel_of_truth.description: EXP-228 correctly identified and fixed two real internal issues. (1) The old P1 did read as a strawman: asserting the dome has 'no equation' linking eclipses to magnetic perturbations was literally false — the dome does publish delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF. The new P1 correctly targets 'no first-principles equation that derives the eclipse magnitude from dome parameters' (the residual critique: the magnitude is imported, not derived). (2) The old P7 set up coverage as the discriminator, but coverage is a shared factor in both models' predictions. The new P7 reframes the discriminator as geographic-latitude FSF vs magnetic-latitude scaling, which IS a real distinction between dome and globe physics.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The rewrite genuinely strengthens our critique but does not save the WIN because the core argument holds: the BOU 2017 -10.9 nT signal is fully predicted by Chapman/Kim & Chang Sq physics without dome parameters, and the absolute-magnitude import from 2017 combined with reuse as the 2026 baseline remains the operative non-discrimination critique. The FSF station-to-station derivation is now conceded and the 2026-08-12 eclipse provides the clean out-of-sample test that will adjudicate whether that derivation is coincidental or meaningful.

advocate_mode.best_defense: Sharp dome defender reading this WIN after EXP-228: 'You have conceded three things that save this WIN: (1) the dome publishes a scaling equation, (2) the station-to-station FSF = H(r)/r derivation reproduces my nine published values at Pearson r = 0.9999, (3) the 2026-08-12 eclipse provides a clean external test. Given those concessions, here is my rebuttal. Your remaining critique is that the absolute magnitude at Boulder is observation-anchored. But *every* physics model anchors somewhere to observation — Chapman 1933 is qualitative, the -10.9 nT quantitative value in the Kim & Chang era came from later model fits against observed eclipse data, so the standard model is equally observation-anchored, just at different calibration sites. Calling one model derived and the other observation-anchored is a category error. More importantly, your P3 asserts any smooth monotonic H(r) produces the same FSF form, but that claim is unsupported in text. The specific exponential H(r) = 8537*exp(-r/8619) was fitted to sun/moon altitude calibration targets — NOT to eclipse magnetic data. Its FSF prediction at the 2026 European stations is therefore a non-trivial out-of-sample output. And your P7 test is asymmetric: my nine FSF numbers are on the table; the magnetic-latitude competitor has no numbers you have pinned. If the 2026 residuals track geographic latitude through my V12 equation more tightly than any post-hoc mag-lat fit can match, that is a genuine scientific result your review cannot pre-dismiss.'
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Two preemptive edits would neutralize this rated-3 defense. First, the 'any smooth monotonic H(r)' claim in P3 needs an in-text demonstration or a softening (see holes_found Hole 4 recommendation). Second, P7 needs to pin down the magnetic-latitude competitor before 2026 data arrives (see holes_found Hole 1 recommendation). Both are already flagged as follow-up EXPs from c6. With those in place, the defender's rebuttal collapses to the 'everyone anchors somewhere' point, which is answerable: standard Sq models calibrate conductivity against a corpus and then predict new events out-of-sample; the dome calibrates against the specific event (2017 BOU) it claims to predict — different epistemic status.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: Checked the three sibling WINs that share the Chapman/Kim & Chang Sq mechanism. WIN-010 finding (post-EXP-228): concedes dome publishes delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF and derives FSF = H(r)/r from its own geometry at Pearson r ~= 0.9999; critiques absolute-magnitude observation-anchoring. WIN-025 finding: 'The dome derives the station-to-station FSF ratio from its own geometry but not the absolute magnitude, which is imported from the 2017 BOU observation and reused as the 2026 baseline input' — consistent framing. WIN-068 finding: 'The dome publishes a scaling equation (delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF) relevant to its 2026 forward projection (WIN-010), but the 1991-2016 Kim & Chang ensemble evaluated here attaches no dome-derived prediction' — consistent framing. All three now share the EXP-220 post-concession vocabulary: scaling derived, magnitude imported. The propagation EXP-228 promised (WIN-068 finding parallel fix) has landed — no cross-WIN contradiction found.

claims_checked: FSF values (0.642-2.075) reproduced from V12 H(r)/r
Pearson r = 0.9999 between claimed and computed FSF
'better than 0.1% at seven of nine stations' precision claim
BOU magnetic latitude ~48 deg inside Kim & Chang 30-50 deg zone
quantitative_verification.computations: Implemented V12 coordinate equation r*tan(theta)=8537*exp(-r/8619) using scipy.optimize.brentq in Python. Solved for r at nine IAGA station geographic latitudes (BOU 40.137, EBR 40.957, SPT 39.550, ESK 55.316, LER 60.138, HAD 50.995, NGK 52.072, SNK 28.321, CLF 48.025, COI 40.222). Computed H(r)/r for each station and normalized to BOU. Compared against the dome's published FSF values from raw-text/03-predictions.txt. Results: BOU 1.000 (pin), EBR 1.0294 vs 1.029 (0.04% err), SPT 0.9794 vs 1.001 (2.16% err), ESK 1.7138 vs 1.722 (0.48% err), LER 2.0657 vs 2.075 (0.45% err), HAD 1.4643 vs 1.471 (0.46% err), NGK 1.5219 vs 1.529 (0.46% err), SNK 0.6391 vs 0.642 (0.44% err), CLF 1.3183 vs 1.325 (0.50% err), COI 1.0030 vs 1.008 (0.49% err). Pearson r (all 10) = 0.999912. Pearson r (excl BOU pin) = 0.999923.
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: The Pearson r = 0.9999 claim reproduces cleanly. The '0.1%/1.8%' precision claim does NOT reproduce — actual distribution is 1/9 at <0.1%, 7/9 at <0.5%, 8/9 at <1.0%, max error 2.16% at SPT. This is a measurable overstatement in P2. EXP-228 deliberately left this for a follow-up micro-patch (per its out_of_scope_deliberately list). Flagged in holes_found as minor. Does not affect the EXP-228 patch itself, which did not touch the precision sentence.

deeper_analysis: EXP-228 delivered what it promised: two targeted edits to detail_verdict_text plus a finding-field replacement, all verified unique before writing and all three patch anchors removed from the current text. Net effect is that our review is now internally consistent between P1 and P2 (the dome-has-no-equation contradiction is gone) and between the summary table finding and the detail_verdict_text (both concede the scaling equation exists and the station-to-station derivation is real). The 2026-08-12 test framing is improved but not complete — we identified the right discriminator (geographic FSF vs magnetic-latitude) but left the competitor unquantified, which is a moderate rhetorical weakness I flagged. Cross-WIN consistency passes: all three Sq-mechanism WINs (010, 025, 068) now share the same post-EXP-220 vocabulary. Quantitative verification confirms the FSF=H(r)/r derivation at Pearson r=0.9999 but shows the '0.1%/1.8%' precision claim is overstated (actual 0.5%/2.2%) — this was deferred by EXP-228 and remains a minor outstanding issue. Advocate mode finds a rated-3 defense hinging on (a) the unsupported 'any smooth monotonic H(r)' claim and (b) the asymmetric P7 test setup; both have preemptive recommendations. Overall: the rewrite is a clear improvement, verdict holds, recommended_action is minor_edit to finish the c6 follow-up holes (3, 4) plus address the two new moderate holes introduced by the rewrite (hole 1 asymmetric test setup; hole 2 implicit linear-coverage assumption).

citations_verified: Chapman (1933) Terr. Mag. Atmos. Electr. - mechanism citation, qualitative, unchanged by EXP-228
Kim & Chang (2018) Adv. Space Res. 61:2040-2054 - quantitative ensemble, unchanged by EXP-228
V12 coordinate equation r*tan(theta) = 8537*exp(-r/8619) reproduces dome's H(r) form from raw-text/04-coordinates.txt
FSF values from raw-text/03-predictions.txt match the values cited in P7 of detail_verdict_text (EBR 1.029, SPT 1.001, ESK 1.722, LER 2.075, HAD 1.471, NGK 1.529, SNK 0.642, CLF 1.325, COI 1.008)
citations_unchecked: IAGA station registry magnetic latitudes for the nine 2026-08-12 stations (checked only geographic latitudes for FSF reproduction; magnetic latitudes would be needed to construct the competing mag-lat predictions recommended in holes_found Hole 1)
NOAA/GFZ Potsdam Kp index archive for 2017-Aug-21 eclipse window (Kp~=3 claim in P4 not directly verified this cycle; accepted from prior cycles)
